macro_segments.title: Opening Discussion: The Ethical and Historical Context of Concerns About Non-Jewish Sexual Behavior

micro_segments.title: Introduction to Daf 23 and Second Perek: Sexual and Physical Concerns with Non-Jews
Lecturer's Personal Response to Charges of Bestiality and Racial Prejudice
The Difference Between Historical Understanding and Contemporary Internalization of Prejudicial Teachings

macro_segments.title: Ravina's Answer: Distinguishing Between Prospective Concern (Lechatchila) and Retrospective Assessment (Bedieved)

micro_segments.title: Ravina's Distinction: We Are Concerned About Future Possibilities But Not About Past Actualities
Explanation of Lechatchila and Bedieved Using the Example of Gittin and the Presumption of Life (Chezkat Chayim)

macro_segments.title: Gemara's Challenge: The Case of a Woman Seized by Non-Jews for Debt Versus for Death Penalty

micro_segments.title: The Mishnah's Prohibition Against Yichud and the Baraita's Two Scenarios: Seized for Money and Seized for Death
The Gemara's Challenge to Ravina: If We're Concerned About Rape, Why Not in the Money Case?
The Gemara's Answer: Economic Self-Interest Protects Against Rape in the Debt Case

macro_segments.title: Tosafot's Expansion: The Ashkenazi Reality of Women in Business and the Distinction Between Brief Yichud and Captivity

micro_segments.title: Tosafot's Astounding Observation: In Ashkenaz, It Is Impossible for Women Not to Be in Yichud With Non-Jews at Some Point
Tosafot's Reference to Women's Status (Isha Hashuvah) in Ashkenaz and Its Ramifications in Halakha
Tosafot's Resolution: Yichud in Business Settings Is Different From Captivity; We Don't Assume Rape After the Fact in Business Settings

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Padat's Answer: The Concern for Bestiality Is a Dispute Between Rabbi Eliezer and the Rabbanan

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Padat Proposes That the Dispute Is Whether We Are Concerned About Acts of Bestiality at All
The Baraita About the Red Heifer (Parah Adumah): Rabbi Eliezer Says Don't Buy From Non-Jews; Chachamim Permit

macro_segments.title: Gemara's Resolution: The Dispute Between Rabbi Eliezer and the Rabbanan Is Whether We Are Concerned About Acts of Bestiality

micro_segments.title: Lecturer's Note: Tosafot Uses This Resolution to Justify Leaving Animals in Non-Jewish Stables in His Time

macro_segments.title: Gemara's First Challenge: Questioning Whether the Dispute Is Really About Bestiality or About Burden on the Animal

micro_segments.title: The Gemara's Initial Hypothesis: Perhaps Everyone Agrees We Don't Worry About Bestiality, and the Dispute Is Only About Putting a Burden on the Animal
The Gemara's Rejection: It Is Irrational to Think Someone Would Risk Losing a Valuable Animal for a Moment's Use
The Gemara's Conclusion: The Only Rational Dispute Is Whether We're Concerned About Bestiality

macro_segments.title: Gemara's Second Hypothesis: Perhaps Everyone Agrees We're Not Concerned About Bestiality, and the Difference Is in the Reasoning for Rabbi Eliezer's Position

micro_segments.title: The Gemara Proposes That Perhaps All Agree There's No Concern for Bestiality, But Rabbi Eliezer Has a Different Reason (The Verse)
The Gemara Challenges This by Noting That Rabbi Eliezer Forbids Buying All Sacrifices From Non-Jews, Not Just the Red Heifer
The Gemara's Conclusion: The Dispute Must Be About Bestiality Concern Since It Applies to All Sacrifices, Not Just Those With a Specific Verse

macro_segments.title: Gemara's Resolution: The Dispute Between Rabbi Eliezer and the Rabbanan Is Whether We Are Concerned About Acts of Bestiality (continued)

micro_segments.title: The Gemara Concludes That Only Rabbi Eliezer Is Concerned About Bestiality; The Rabbanan Are Not Concerned

macro_segments.title: Exploration of How Bestiality Invalidates a Red Heifer Despite Its Non-Sacrificial Status

micro_segments.title: The Gemara Asks: How Can an Act of Bestiality Invalidate a Red Heifer If It's Not a Sacrifice?
The Gemara's Inference: Perhaps the Red Heifer Has the Same Sanctity as a Sacrifice
Alternative Possibility: Perhaps a Red Heifer Only Has Temple Sanctity, Not Sacrificial Sanctity, So Bestiality Shouldn't Invalidate It
The Gemara's Answer: The Torah Calls the Red Heifer a 'Sin Offering' (Chatat), So Invalidities of a Sin Offering Apply to It

macro_segments.title: The Question of C-Section Animals and Rabbi Shimon's Position on the Red Heifer

micro_segments.title: The Gemara Tests Its Theory: If the Red Heifer Has Sacrificial Invalidities, Shouldn't a C-Section Animal Be Invalid?
The Baraita: According to the Chachamim, a C-Section Red Heifer Is Invalid; According to Rabbi Shimon, It Is Valid
The Gemara's Initial Explanation: Rabbi Shimon Generally Doesn't Distinguish Between C-Section and Vaginal Birth
The Gemara's Problem: Rabbi Yochanan Says Even Rabbi Shimon Agrees a C-Section Animal Can't Be Used for Normal Sacrifices

macro_segments.title: The Gemara's Final Answer: The Red Heifer Is Invalidated by 'Blemish' (Mum), Including Ritual Blemishes Like Bestiality and Idolatry

micro_segments.title: The Gemara's Resolution: The Red Heifer Is Invalidated by Physical Blemish; Rabbi Shimon Extends This to Ritual Blemishes
Acts of Bestiality and Idolatry as Ritual Blemishes Derived From the Verse About Corruption (Hashchasah)
The Tannaitic Midrash From Rabbi Yishmael's School: 'Corruption' Refers to Sexual Perversion and Idolatry

macro_segments.title: Shila's Interpretation: Rabbi Eliezer's Reason Is the Verse About Taking the Red Heifer From the Israelites

micro_segments.title: Shila Teaches That Rabbi Eliezer's Reasoning Is Based on the Verse 'Speak to the Israelites and Have Them Take a Red Heifer'
The Gemara's Challenge: If This Verse Limits the Red Heifer to Jews, Does the Same Apply to the Donations (Terumah) for the Temple?

macro_segments.title: The Story of Dama ben Netina: A Non-Jew's Exemplary Filial Piety and the Purchase of Precious Stones for the Ephod

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Eliezer Cites a Non-Jewish Example of Filial Piety to Illustrate the Virtue Beyond Legal Obligation
The Story: Dama Ben Netina Refused to Wake His Father to Sell Precious Stones for the Ephod at a Huge Profit
The Problem: If the Verse Limits Taking Things for the Tabernacle to Israelites, How Could They Buy the Ephod Stones From Dama?

macro_segments.title: The Gemara's Unresolved Cliffhanger: The Apparent Contradiction in the Verse's Limiting Language

micro_segments.title: The Gemara Leaves Unresolved How Rabbi Eliezer Can Permit Buying Ephod Stones From a Non-Jew While Forbidding the Red Heifer
